checks.a_analysis: The output text for the sections visible in the snippet (Sections 1, 2, 3, 13, 24, and 25) exactly matches the content of the corresponding pages in the source PDF. There are no signs of hallucinated or invented content. The omitted sections (4-12 and 14-23) are explicitly noted as omitted for space, which is expected behavior.

No contact information such as phone numbers, emails, or physical addresses exists in the source document. The QR code on page 24 is correctly captured as an image element. No invented URLs were added.
The JSON metadata specifies a page count of 25, which matches the source document. Every page from the original is accounted for in the structural flow of the JSON sections.
The table in Section 13 (representing the multi-column layout of page 13) accurately contains all five categories and their associated descriptions. No data rows or columns are missing.

checks.a_analysis: I examined the provided JSON and the source PDF. Each slide's unique content appears only once in the corresponding section of the output. There are no duplicated paragraphs or sections found in the provided fragments.

The output correctly identifies slide titles as H1 (e.g., 'Agenda Items', 'Use Cases'). Subtitles or text-box labels are treated as paragraphs or table headers depending on the layout, which is an acceptable adjustment for slide-to-document conversion to maintain structure without skipping levels.

On page 13 (index 12 in the sections array), the output transforms a 5-column layout of text boxes into a structured table. The header row correctly captures the column labels ('Personal Work-in-Progress', etc.) and the data row contains the corresponding descriptions. This is a valid structural interpretation of a grid layout.

The output sections follow the slide sequence of the source PDF. Within the slides, the reading order correctly progresses from the title to the content elements. The table on page 13 reads from left to right as organized in the source.
The 'Agenda Items' on page 2 are correctly identified as an unordered list in the output, preserving the items and the list structure found in the source PDF.
The source document does not contain explicit hyperlink text. The output does not contain any injected or broken links.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output successfully maintains the visual organization of the source presentation. While fixed layouts from the slides (like side-by-side text and images) are converted to a stacked vertical layout in the HTML, this is a standard and acceptable practice for responsive web design. The 5-column 'Use Cases' on page 13 are appropriately rendered as a table to preserve their structured relationship. Information remains in the same relative order as the source.

The document contains a multi-column layout on page 13 which has been converted into an HTML table. The rendered table is visually sound, with clear column separation and readable content. No other tables are present in the source.

Two meaningful images are missing from the HTML output. First, the Microsoft logo on page 1 is missing and replaced by the bracketed alt text '[Microsoft logo in the top left corner]'. Second, the QR code on page 24, which is the primary functional content of that slide (Follow-Up), is entirely absent from the HTML rendering, leaving an empty space between the section heading and the next section.
checks.b_evidence_source: Microsoft logo in the top-left corner of page 1; large QR code in the center of page 24.
checks.b_evidence_output: The text '[Microsoft logo in the top left corner]' appears as plain text below the subtitle. The section 'NC DIT Innovation Summit Follow-Up' is empty below its heading.
checks.b_evidence_location: Microsoft logo: Page 1 / Title section; QR code: Page 24 / Follow-Up section.

checks.a_analysis: There is no evidence of body content duplication. Section headings appear in the navigation area and the main body as intended for accessibility, but paragraphs and tables are not repeated.
